title: Check if Linked List is Palindrome

content.problemStatement:
Chef has been given a singly linked list containing integers.

He wants to check whether the given linked list forms a **palindrome** — i.e., it reads the same forwards and backwards.

Your task is to help Chef determine if the linked list is a palindrome.

Function DeclarationFunction NameisPalindromeisPalindromeisPalindrome – This function checks whether a singly linked list is a palindrome.

Parameters
headheadhead : A pointer to the head node of the singly linked list.
Return Value
Returns truetruetrue if the linked list is a palindrome.
Returns falsefalsefalse otherwise.
Constraints
1≤N≤1051 \leq N \leq 10^51≤N≤105, where NNN is the number of nodes in the linked list.
0≤0 \leq0≤ Value stored in each node ≤9\leq 9≤9
The linked list is singly linked
The input and output formats provided below are only for testing with custom inputs. You only need to complete the core logic function.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the linked list.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the values of the linked list nodes in order.


Output Format

Print YESYESYES if the linked list is a palindrome.
Otherwise, print NONONO.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the linked list. The second line contains N space‑separated integers (each between 0 and 9) representing the node values in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing "YES" if the linked list is a palindrome, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First list [1,2,3,2,1] reads the same forward and backward → YES. Second list [1,2,3,4] is not a palindrome → NO. A single node list [7] is always a palindrome → YES.
The first list is symmetric around the centre (9,8,7,7,8,9) → YES. The second list differs at the last element (1,2,2,3) → NO.
Odd length palindrome with central element 8; the sequence reads identically from both ends.

solution.approach: For each test case we traverse the list with two pointers: 'slow' moves one step, 'fast' moves two steps. When 'fast' reaches the end, 'slow' is at the middle. We reverse the second half of the list starting from slow->next. Then we compare the first half and the reversed second half node by node. If all corresponding values match, the list is a palindrome. The reversal is done in‑place, so the algorithm uses O(1) extra memory. Optionally we could restore the original order, but it is not required for the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – each node is visited a constant number of times (finding middle, reversing, comparing).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space – only a few pointer variables are used.
